:::{#def-potential-mean}

#### Potential mean

The **potential mean** (or **mean potential outcome**) under treatment $A = a$ is
the expected value of the potential outcome $Y^a$:

$$\mu_a \eqdef \E{Y^a}$$

The potential mean is what the average outcome would be
if the entire population were assigned treatment $a$.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-potential-mean}

#### Illustration: potential mean

Suppose, hypothetically, that if every man in the WCGS cohort
had exhibited Type A behavior pattern ($a = 1$),
the fraction who would go on to develop CHD by 1969
were close to the fraction actually observed
among the men who really did have that behavior pattern,
$\pi(1) = `r round(pi1_direct, 3)`$ (@exm-observed-marginal-risk-direct).
Under that supposition, the potential mean is
$\mu_1 = \E{Y^1} = `r round(pi1_direct, 3)`$.

:::

For a binary outcome,
the potential mean is a probability,
which we give its own name:

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#def-causal-marginal-risk}

#### Causal marginal risk

For a binary outcome $Y \in \{0, 1\}$,
the potential mean (@def-potential-mean) is a probability,
which we call the **causal marginal risk**
(also: **potential probability**, **potential marginal risk**)
under exposure level $a$:

$$\pi_a \eqdef \Pr(Y^a = 1)$$

:::

We use the term **causal marginal risk** throughout.

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-causal-marginal-risk-illustration}

#### Illustration: causal marginal risk

Continuing @exm-potential-mean,
under that same supposition
the causal marginal risk is
$\pi_1 = \Pr(Y^1 = 1) = `r round(pi1_direct, 3)`$ —
the fraction who would develop CHD
if every man in the cohort had Type A behavior pattern.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#thm-causal-marginal-risk-equals-mean}

#### Causal marginal risk equals potential mean

The matching values in @exm-potential-mean and @exm-causal-marginal-risk-illustration
are no coincidence:
the causal marginal risk (@def-causal-marginal-risk)
is a special case of the potential mean (@def-potential-mean):

$$\pi_a = \E{Y^a}$$

::: {.proof}

Since $Y^a$ is binary ($Y^a \in \{0, 1\}$):

$$
\ba
\E{Y^a}
&= 1 \cdot \Pr(Y^a = 1) + 0 \cdot \Pr(Y^a = 0)
&& \text{[expectation of a binary variable]}
\\
&= \Pr(Y^a = 1)
&& \text{[arithmetic]}
\\
&= \pi_a
&& \text{[definition of causal marginal risk]}
\ea
$$

:::

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-causal-marginal-risk-as-mean}

#### Illustration: causal marginal risk equals potential mean

With $\pi_1 = `r round(pi1_direct, 3)`$ as in @exm-causal-marginal-risk-illustration,
reading the potential mean as an expectation gives the same value:

$$
\E{Y^1} = 1 \cdot `r round(pi1_direct, 3)` + 0 \cdot `r round(1 - pi1_direct, 3)`
= `r round(pi1_direct, 3)` = \pi_1.
$$

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

Under causal identification assumptions
(see [Consistency](causal-inference.qmd#def-consistency),
[Conditional Exchangeability](causal-inference.qmd#def-exchangeability),
and [Positivity](causal-inference.qmd#def-positivity)),
the causal marginal risk $\pi_a$ (@def-causal-marginal-risk)
can be expressed in terms of observed data:

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_thm-causal-marginal-risk.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-causal-marginal-risk}

#### Causal marginal risk by standardization

Continuing @exm-observed-marginal-risk
with the same age-specific risks
$\pi(1, 0) = `r round(pi1_z0, 3)`$ and $\pi(1, 1) = `r round(pi1_z1, 3)`$,
now weight by the age distribution of the **whole** WCGS population
rather than just the Type A men:

```{r}
#| label: wcgs-causal-marginal-risk-strata
#| code-fold: true

p_z1_pop <- mean(wcgs_clean$age_ge50)
```

$\Pr(Z = 1) = `r round(p_z1_pop, 3)`$ of the whole population is 50 or older —
a smaller share than among the Type A men
($\Pr(Z = 1 \mid A = 1) = `r round(p_z1_a1, 3)`$),
since behavior pattern and age are associated in this cohort.
Then @thm-causal-marginal-risk gives:

$$
\ba
\pi_1
&= \E{\pi(1, Z)}
&& \text{[causal marginal risk theorem]}
\\
&= (1 - `r round(p_z1_pop, 3)`)(`r round(pi1_z0, 3)`)
+ (`r round(p_z1_pop, 3)`)(`r round(pi1_z1, 3)`)
&& \text{[substitute stratum risks and weights]}
\\
&= `r round((1 - p_z1_pop) * pi1_z0 + p_z1_pop * pi1_z1, 3)`
&& \text{[arithmetic]}
\ea
$$

This causal marginal risk differs (slightly) from the observed marginal risk
$\pi(1) = `r round(pi1_direct, 3)`$
because the Type A men skew somewhat older
than the WCGS population as a whole.

:::

We can now place the observed and causal marginal risks side by side:

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_rem-observed-vs-causal-marginal-risk.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#cor-observed-equals-causal}

#### Observed and causal marginal risks coincide without confounding

Assume [Consistency](causal-inference.qmd#def-consistency),
[Conditional Exchangeability](causal-inference.qmd#def-exchangeability),
[Positivity](causal-inference.qmd#def-positivity),
and $Z \ind A$.
Then the observed and causal marginal risks are equal:

$$\pi(a) = \pi_a$$

::: {.proof}

When $Z \ind A$ the conditional and marginal distributions of $Z$ coincide,
$\red{f_{Z \mid A}(z \mid a)} = \red{f_Z(z)}$,
so @thm-observed-marginal-risk and @thm-causal-marginal-risk give:

$$
\ba
\pi(a)
&= \E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)} \mid A = a}
&& \text{[observed marginal risk theorem]}
\\
&= \E{\teal{\pi(a, Z)}}
&& \text{[no confounding]}
\\
&= \pi_a
&& \text{[causal marginal risk theorem]}
\ea
$$

:::

:::

Under complete randomization of $A$,
the design delivers $Z \ind A$ together with the other two identification assumptions
as parallel consequences —
conditional exchangeability (randomized $A$ is independent of the potential
outcomes, hence also independent given $Z$) and positivity (every level has
positive probability in each stratum) —
so consistency and randomization alone suffice
[@hernan2020causal, Chapters 2-3].
(Marginal independence $Z \ind A$ on its own does not imply exchangeability;
randomization is the stronger design assumption that yields all three.)

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exm-observed-equals-causal}

#### Illustration: coincidence under randomization

Take the same age-specific risks $\pi(1, 0) = `r round(pi1_z0, 3)`$
and $\pi(1, 1) = `r round(pi1_z1, 3)`$ of @exm-observed-marginal-risk,
but now suppose $A$ had been randomized,
so the exposed would carry the population's age distribution
instead of the actual, older-skewing one:
$\Pr(Z = 1 \mid A = 1) = \Pr(Z = 1) = `r round(p_z1_pop, 3)`$.
Then the observed marginal risk would be

$$
\ba
\pi(1)
&= \E{\pi(1, Z) \mid A = 1}
&& \text{[observed marginal risk theorem]}
\\
&= (1 - `r round(p_z1_pop, 3)`)(`r round(pi1_z0, 3)`)
+ (`r round(p_z1_pop, 3)`)(`r round(pi1_z1, 3)`)
&& \text{[substitute randomized weights]}
\\
&= `r round((1 - p_z1_pop) * pi1_z0 + p_z1_pop * pi1_z1, 3)`
&& \text{[arithmetic]}
\ea
$$

equal to the causal marginal risk
$\pi_1 = `r round((1 - p_z1_pop) * pi1_z0 + p_z1_pop * pi1_z1, 3)`$
of @exm-causal-marginal-risk,
exactly as @cor-observed-equals-causal predicts.

:::
